title: Will The AI Boom Prove Inflationary Or Deflationary?
text:
Will The AI Boom Prove Inflationary Or Deflationary?

Will Denyer, Tan Kai Xian

Gavekal Research | 13 Jul 2026

A key question for investors and policymakers is whether the maturing artificial intelligence infrastructure boom will prove inflationary or deflationary for consumer prices. The conventional view is that it is inflationary in its early stages, driven by heavy capital spending and high expectations, before becoming deflationary as productivity gains materialize. That framework is compelling and worth unpacking. The challenge is determining when the cycle will transition from its inflationary phase to its deflationary phase.The importance of this issue has been reinforced by Federal Reserve chair Kevin Warsh’s decision to launch a series of task forces to examine issues shaping the future conduct of monetary policy, including AI’s impact on productivity, employment and inflation. If AI materially raises the economy’s productive capacity, it could allow stronger growth with less inflationary pressure, fundamentally changing how policymakers think about interest rates. Investors therefore need to assess not only whether AI will boost productivity, but also when those gains will begin to outweigh the inflationary forces created by the investment boom itself (see Warsh’s Task Forces).
